question: An $\alpha$-particle of energy ' $E$ ' is liberated during the decay of a nucleus of mass number 236. The total energy released in this process is
explanation:
In decay of nucleus, momentum remains conserved.
As, $K E=\dfrac{p^{2}}{2 m} \Rightarrow K E \propto \dfrac{1}{m}$
$\therefore \dfrac{(K E)_{232}}{(K E)_{\alpha}}=\dfrac{4}{232}=\dfrac{1}{58} \therefore(K E)_{232}=\dfrac{E}{58}$
$\therefore$ Total energy released in this process,
$Q=(K E)_{\alpha}+(K E)_{232}=E+\dfrac{E}{58}=\dfrac{59 E}{58}$
subject: Physics

question: A nucleus at rest breaks into two nuclear parts which have their velocities ratio equal to $2: 1$. What will be the ratio of their radii of the nuclei?
explanation:
According to the law of conservation linear momentum,
$\begin{aligned}& m_{1} v_{1}=m_{2} v_{2} \\& \therefore \dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}=\dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{2} \quad\left(\text { given }, \dfrac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=2\right)\end{aligned}$
Since, we know
$\begin{aligned}& R=R \cdot(A)^{1 / 3} \\& A \approx m\end{aligned}$
Thus, $\dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{m_{1}}{m_{2}}\right)^{1 / 3}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}$
$\therefore R_{1}: R_{2}=1: 2^{1 / 3}$
subject: Physics

question: Tritium is an isotope of hydrogen whose nucleus Triton contains 2 neutrons and 1 proton. Free neutrons decay into  $p + \bar e + \bar v.$ If one of the neutrons in Triton decays, it would transform into $H{e^3}$ nucleus. This does not happen. This is because
explanation: Tritium  $ \to {}_1{H^3}.$ The nucleus contain 1 proton and 2 neutrons. If one neutron decays  $n \to p + {e^ - } + \overline v ,$ the nucleus will have 2 protons and one neutron, i.e., tritium will transform into ${}_2H{e^3}.$ This does not happen because triton energy is less than that of  ${}_2H{e^3}$ nucleus, i.e., transformation is not allowed energetically.

options.text: The electron created in the beta decay process cannot remain in the nucleus.
Because free neutrons decay due to external perturbations which is absent in a triton nucleus.
Triton energy is less than that of a  nucleus.
Both the neutrons in triton have to decay simultaneously resulting in a nucleus with 3 protons, which is not a $H{e^3}$ nucleus.

subject: Physics

question: A nucleus at rest splits into two nuclear parts having radii in the ratio 1:2. Their velocities are in the ratio
explanation:
Let ${A_1}$ and ${A_2}$ be the mass number of the two nuclear parts. Their radii are given by
${R_1} = {R_0}A_1^{1/3}\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
and ${R_2} = {R_0}A_2^{1/3}\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing eqn. (1) by eqn(2), we get
$\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{1/3}}or\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}}} \right)^3}$
As $\frac{{{R_1}}}{{{R_2}}} = \frac{1}{2}$ (given)
$\therefore \frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3} = \frac{1}{8}$
Hence the ratio of their masses is
$\frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}} = \frac{1}{8}$ (3)
($\therefore $ mass is approximately proprtional to $A$)
According to law of conservation of linear momentum
magnitude of ${{\vec p}_1} = $ magnitude of ${{\vec p}_2}$
i.e., ${m_1}{v_1} = {m_2}{v_2}$
or $\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}} = \frac{8}{1}$ (using (3))
subject: Physics

question: A nucleus of mass 20$u$ emits a $\gamma $ photon of energy 6 $MeV$. If the emission is assumed to occur when nucleus is free and at rest, then the nucleus will have kinetic energy nearest to ( take $1u = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 27}}\,kg$)
explanation:
Mass of nucleus, $M = 20u$
${E_1} = 6MeV = 6 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 13}}J$
Using momentum conservation principle
$0 = Mv + \frac{{{E_\gamma }}}{c}$
$\therefore v = - \frac{{{E_\gamma }}}{{Mc}} = - \frac{{6 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 13}}}}{{20 \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 27}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}$
$ = - {10^5}m{s^{ - 1}}$
$K$.$E$ of nucleus, $K = \frac{1}{2}M{v^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 27}} \times {\left( {{{10}^5}} \right)^2} = 16 \times {10^{ - 17}}J$
$ = \frac{{16 \times {{10}^{ - 17}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 16}}}}keV = 1\,\,keV$
subject: Physics

question: A radioactive nucleus of mass $M$ emits a photon of frequency $v$ and the nucleus recoils. The recoil energy will be
explanation:
Momentum of a photon $p = \frac{{hv}}{c}$
Hence, recoil energy of the nucleus is $E = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2M}}$ ($M$ is the mass of the nucleus)
$\therefore \quad E = \frac{{{{\left( {\frac{{hv}}{c}} \right)}^2}}}{{2M}} \Rightarrow E = \frac{{{h^2}{v^2}}}{{2M{c^2}}}$
subject: Physics

question: A nucleus  $_Z{X^A}$ emits an  $\alpha $ particle with velocity $v$. The recoil speed of the daughter nucleus is
explanation:
According to law of conservation of linear momentum
$0 = {m_D}{v_D} + {m_\alpha }{v_\alpha }$($\because $ The parent nucleus is initially at rest)
Where subscripts $D$ and $\alpha $ represent daughter nucleus and $\alpha $-particle respectively
$0 = \left( {A - 4} \right){v_D} + 4v$ ( $\because $ Mass of a nucleus is approximately proportional to its mass number)
${v_D} = - \frac{{4v}}{{\left( {A - 4} \right)}}$
Negative sign shows the recoil of the daughter nucleus
subject: Physics

question: A stationary Thorium nucleus $(A = 200,Z = 90)$ emits an alpha particle with kinetic energy. What is the kinetic energy of the recoiling nucleus?
explanation:
The reaction can be represented by
$_{90}^{220}Th \to _2^4He + _{88}^{216}Ra$
By conservation of momentum,
$ \Rightarrow {m_\alpha }{v_\alpha } = {m_R}{v_R}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{v_R}}}{{{v_\alpha }}} = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_R}}} = \frac{4}{{216}} = \frac{1}{{54}}$
The kinetic energy of $Ra,\,{E_R}$, is related to the kinetic energy of alpha particle ${E_\alpha }$ by
$\frac{{{E_R}}}{{{E_\alpha }}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}{m_R}v_R^2}}{{\frac{1}{2}{m_\alpha }v_\alpha ^2}} = \left( {\frac{{{m_R}}}{{{m_\alpha }}}} \right){\left( {\frac{{{v_R}}}{{{v_\alpha }}}} \right)^2} = \left( {\frac{{{m_R}}}{{{m_\alpha }}}} \right){\left( {\frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_R}}}} \right)^2}$
$ = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_R}}} = \frac{1}{{54}}$
$\therefore {E_R} = \frac{{{E_\alpha }}}{{54}}$
subject: Physics

question: A heavy nucleus at rest breaks into two fragments which fly off with velocities in the ratio 8:1. The ratio of radii of the fragments is
explanation:
Let ${m_1}$ and ${m_2}$are the masses of two fragments.
By conservation of momentum ${m_1}{v_1} = {m_2}{v_2}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \frac{8}{1} = \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}} = \frac{{m{A_2}}}{{m{A_1}}} = \frac{{{A_2}}}{{{A_1}}}$ (1)
Where $m$ is the mass of each nucleon.
Also from $r \propto {A^{1/3}} \Rightarrow \frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{1/3}} = {\left( {\frac{1}{8}} \right)^{1/3}} = \frac{1}{2}.$
subject: Physics

question: Which one of the following statements is correct?
explanation: The nucleons inside the nucleus are very strongly bound and energies of few  $MeV$  are needed to separate a nucleon from the nucleus. Hence, a minimum energy is required to split the protons and neutrons and that energy is known as the binding energy of the nucleus. If, there are $Z$ number of protons and $N$ number of neutrons and mass of the nucleus is $M(A, Z)$, then binding energy $E_{B}=Z m_{p} c^{2}+N m_{n} c^{2}-M(A, Z) c^{2}>0$ where $m_{p}$ and $m_{n}$ are the masses of protons and neutrons, respectively. Hence, we can conclude that, the mass of the nucleus must be less than the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons. So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: The mass of the nucleus must be less than the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons.
The mass of the nucleus must be equal to the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons.
The mass of the nucleus must be greater than the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons.
The mass of the nucleus must be equal to only the masses of the constituent neutrons or protons .

subject: Physics
topic: Mass Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy
parent_topic: Mass Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy

question:
What is the binding energy of ${ }_{14}^{29} {Si}$, whose atomic mass is $28.976495 u$ ?
Mass of proton $=1.007276 u$
Mass of neutron $=1.008664 u$
(neglect the electron mass)
(assume, $1 u=931.5 {MeV}$)
explanation:
Given, mass of ${ }_{14}^{29} {Si}=28.976495 u$
Now, mass of 14 protons
$=14 \times$ mass of 1 proton
$=14 \times 1.007276=14.101864 u$
Also, mass of $29-14=15$ neutrons $=15 \times$ mass of $I$ neutron
$=15 \times 1.008664=15.12996 u$
Total mass of constituents of Si atom
$=14.101864 u+15.12996 u=29.231824 u$
So, mass defect $\Delta M=$ mass of constituents $ - $ mass of atom
$=29.231824 u-28.976495 u=0.255329 u$
Binding energy $=$ Energy equivalent of mass defect $=\Delta M \times 931.5 {MeV}$
$=0.255329 \times 931.5=237.84 {MeV}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
topic: Mass Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy
parent_topic: Mass Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy

$=\dfrac{28.6}{7.14}=4$

$\Rightarrow Q=n E=\dfrac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{235} \times 200 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}$

question: The binding energy per nucleon of dueterium and helium nuclei are $1.1\,MeV$ and $7\,MeV$ respectively. When two deuterium nuclei fuse to form a helium nucleus the energy released in the fusion is:

The binding energy per nucleon of a deuterium $ = 1.1\,MeV$.
$\therefore$ Total binding energy $ = 4 \times 1.1 = 4.4\,MeV$.
The binding energy per nucleon of a helium nuclei $ = 7\,MeV$.
$\therefore$ Total binding energy $ = 4 \times 7 = 28\,MeV$.
Hence, energy released.
$\Delta E = (28 - 4.4)\,MeV = 23.6\,MeV.$

question:
Assertion: The binding energy per nucleon, for nuclei with atomic mass number $A>100$, decrease with $A$.

Reason: The nuclear forces are weak for heavier nuclei.
explanation:
It is a well-established fact that the binding energy per nucleon decreases for heavy nuclei $(\mathrm{A}>100)$. This phenomenon is primarily due to the increasing influence of the Coulomb repulsion between protons as the number of protons in the nucleus increases. The Coulomb repulsion counteracts the attractive nuclear force, leading to a decrease in the binding energy per nucleon. The nuclear forces, often referred to as the strong nuclear force, are responsible for holding the nucleus together. The strong nuclear force is not weak for heavier nuclei; it is the dominant force that overcomes the electrostatic repulsion between protons and maintains nuclear stability. The decrease in binding energy per nucleon for heavy nuclei is primarily due to the increasing Coulomb repulsion between protons, not the weakness of the nuclear forces.
The binding energy per nucleon decreases in heavy nuclei due to the Coulomb repulsion, not the weakness of the nuclear forces. So, the assertion is correct, but the reason provided is incorrect. So option (3) is correct

question:
Mass numbers of the elements $A,B,C$ and $D$ are 30, 60, 90 and 120 respectively. The specific binding energy of them are $5\,MeV,8.5\,MeV,8\,MeV$ and $7\,MeV$ respectively. Then, in which of the following reactions energy is released? $(i){\rm{ }}D \to 2B\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(ii){\rm{ }}C \to B + A$
(iii) $B \to 2A$
explanation:
If the energy is to be released then the products should have more $B$.$E$ (More stable) than the reactants.
$D \to 2B$
$120 \times 7 \to 2 \times 60 \times 8.5\,\,\left( {\operatorname{possible} } \right)$
$C \to B + A$
$90 \times 8 \to 60 \times 8.5 + 30 \times 5\,\,\left( {\operatorname{not} \,possible} \right)$
$B \to 2A$
$60 \times 8.5 \to 2 \times 30 \times 5\,\,\left( {not\,possible} \right)$
In only reaction (i) $D \to 2B$ the $B$.$E$ is more for products.

question: Assume that a neutron breaks into a proton and 	an electron. The energy absorbed during this process is: (mass of neutron  	$ = 1.6725 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg,$ mass of proton $ = 1.6725 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg,$ mass of electron $ = 9 \times {10^{ - 31}}kg$).
explanation:
${}_0^1n \to {}_1^1P{ + _{ - 1}}{e^0} + \bar v + Q$
The mass defect during the process
$\Delta m = {m_n} - {m_H} - {m_e} = 1.6725 \times {10^{27}}$
$ - (1.6725 \times {10^{ - 27}} + 9 \times {10^{ - 31}}kg)$
$ = - 9 \times {10^{ - 31}}kg$
Here $\Delta m$ is $( - )ve$ so the reaction absorbs energy.The energy absorbed during the process
$E = \Delta m{c^2}$
$E = 9 \times {10^{ - 31}} \times 9 \times {10^{16}} = 81 \times {10^{ - 15}}{\text{joules}}$
$E = \frac{{81 \times {{10}^{ - 15}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}} = 0.511{\mkern 1mu} $ $Mev$
The correct answer is $ - 0.511\,MeV$

question: A nucleus with mass number 220 initially at rest emits an $\alpha $-particle. If the $Q$ value of the reaction is $5.5\,MeV$, calculate the kinetic energy of the $\alpha $-particle:
explanation:
Linear momentum is conserved.
$\therefore {p_1} = {p_2}$
But $p = \sqrt {2mK} $ where $K = $ kinetic energy
$\therefore \sqrt {2(216m){K_1}} = \sqrt {2(4m){K_2}} $
$216{K_1} = 4{K_2} \Rightarrow {K_2} = 54{K_1}$ (1)
Given ${K_1} + {K_2} = 5.5MeV$ (2)
From (1) and (2)
or ${K_1} = \frac{1}{{10}}MeV$ and ${K_2} = 5.4MeV$
$\therefore $ Kinetic energy of $\alpha $ particle $ = 5.4\,MeV$.

question: Which of the following statement is incorrect?
explanation:
According to question we have to find incorrect option but
Option (1) According to Noether's theorem, law of conservation of energy is associated with symmetry of nature. As a result of which, the amount of conserved quantity of any isolated system doesn't change, until some in flow/out flow of physical quantities takes place. Hence, option (1) is the correct.
Option (2) As we know that, the strength of fundamental force i. e. in order of
(1) strong nuclear force $=1$
(2) electromagnetic force $=10^{-2}$
(3) weak nuclear force $=10^{-7}$
(4) gravity $=10^{-38}$
$\therefore$ Strong nuclear force and electromagnetic force $>$ weak nuclear force $>$ gravitational force. Hence, option (2) is the incorrect.
Option (3) As we know that, the law of conservation of mass, momentum, energy etc. can only be easily observed by experimental observations. Hence, option (3) is the correct.
Option (4) Since there are two types of nuclear reaction i.e. nuclear fission & fusion and in both cases we find generation of energy due to breaking of heavy nuclei ($U_{235}$) or by fusion of two light nuclei i. e. ($H_{1}^{2}$ and $\left. H_{1}^{3}\right)$, respectively. In each nuclear reaction mass is converted into energy. So, the correct option is (2).

options.text: Conservation laws have deep connection with symmetries of nature.
Weak nuclear force is weakest among all fundamental forces of nature.
A conservation law is hypothesis based on observations and experiments.
In a nuclear process mass gets converted to energy or vice-versa.

question:
Sun radiates energy in all directions. The average energy received at earth is $1.4\,kW/{m^2}$. The average distance between the earth and the sun is $1.5 \times {10^{11}}\,m$. If this energy is released by conservation of mass into energy, then the mass lost per day by the sun is approximately
(Use 1 day $=86400\,sec$ )
explanation:
The sun radiates energy in all directions in a sphere. At a distance ${R}$, the energy received per unit area per second is $1.4\,kJ$ (given).
Therefore, the energy released in area ${4 \pi R^{2}}$ per second is ${=1400 \times 4 \pi R^{2}}$ joule. The energy released per day ${=1400 \times 4 \pi R^{2} \times 86400 J}$,
where ${R=1.5 \times 10^{11} {~m}}$. Thus,
$\Delta E=1400 \times 4 \times 3.14 \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{11}\right)^{2} \times 86400$
The equivalent mass is
${\Delta m=\Delta E / c^{2}}$
$\Delta m = \frac{{\left[ \begin{array}{l} 1400 \times 4 \times 3.14 \times \\ {\left( {1.5 \times {{10}^{11}}} \right)^2} \times 86400 \end{array} \right]}}{{9 \times {{10}^{16}}}}$
${\Delta m=3.8 \times 10^{14} {~kg}}$
subject: Physics

The mass-energy equivalence principle was experimentally verified through nuclear reactions.
In a chemical reaction, mass effect is the amount mass converted into energy.
explanation: Statement A is true as the mass-energy equivalence principle has been confirmed experimentally in nuclear reactions. Statement B is false because even in chemical reactions, mass is converted into energy, though the amount is much smaller compared to nuclear reactions. Correct option is (2)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.
Statement A is true, but Statement B is false.
Statement A is false, but Statement B is true.
Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

subject: Physics
topic: Mass Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The nuclear force between two nucleons is attractive for distances larger than about 0.8 femtometres and repulsive for shorter distances.

Reason: This behaviour of nuclear force ensures that nucleons can be close enough to be bound within the nucleus without collapsing into each other.
explanation: The nuclear force exhibits a short-range nature, being attractive at distances greater than about 0.8 femtometres and becoming repulsive at shorter distances. This characteristic allows nucleons to be bound within the nucleus with sufficient strength while preventing them from collapsing into a more compact space due to the repulsive force that arises at very short distances. The attractive nuclear force is essential for overcoming the electrostatic repulsive forces between the positively charged protons in the nucleus, enabling the nucleus to exist as a stable, bound system. The repulsive component at very short distances prevents the nucleons from being compressed into an infinitely small point, which would not be physically meaningful, hence contributing to the stability and finite size of the nucleus. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and    Reason is the correct explanation of the  Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but  Reason is not the correct explanation of the    Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

question: Nuclear fission and fusion can be explained on the basis of
explanation:
Nuclear fission
When a heavy nucleus is splits into two or more lighter nuclei.
Nuclear fusion
When two or more light nuclei adds together to form a heavy nuclei.
These two above mention phenomenon can be explained using Einstein's mass energy relation.
$E=\Delta m c^{2}$
where $\Delta m$ is the mass defect [mass of reactant - mass of product]
This mass is converted into energy. So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: In the following nuclear reaction $X$ is ${ }_{13} A l^{27}+{ }_{2} H e^{4} \rightarrow{ }_{0}^{n^{1}}+X$
explanation:
Given, ${ }_{13} A l^{27}+{ }_{2} H e^{4} \rightarrow{ }_{0} n^{1}+{ }_{b} X^{a}$
According to law of conservation of mass number
$27+4=1+a$
$a=30$
So mass number of element is 30.
Now, the conservation of atomic number
$13+2=0+b \Rightarrow b=15$
So, the atomic number of element is 15.
Hence, the element $X$ is ${ }_{15} P^{30}$.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: If $F_{1}$ and $F_{2}$ are the relative strengths of the gravitational and weak nuclear forces  respectively, then $F_{2} / F_{1}$ is nearly
explanation:
Given,
Gravitational forces $=F_{1}$
Weak nuclear forces $=F_{2}$
Gravitational force is the weakest fundamental force in the nature. It acts between bodies with masses.
Weak nuclear force is the force which allows proton to turn into neutron and vice-versa through beta decay. The ratio of strength of weak nuclear force to gravitational force is $10^{26}$.
So, the correct option is (4).

question: The energy equivalent to a mass of  $1{\text{ }}kg$  is
explanation:
Energy equivalence of a mass is given by $E=m c^{2} \quad[m$ is mass of object $c$ is speed of light] $m$ is given as $1{\text{ }}kg$ and $ = 3 \times {10^8}\,m/s$
$E = 1 \times {\left( {3 \times {{10}^8}} \right)^2}J = 9 \times {10^{16}}J$
So, the correct option is (3).

explanation:

question: In the nuclear fission of one nucleus of $U^{235}$ the energy released is  $188{\text{ }}MeV.$  The energy released in the nuclear fission of  $235{\text{ }}g$  of   is   $U^{235}$ nearly$\left(\right. $Avogadro number $\left. =6.02 \times 10^{23} {~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
Energy released in one fission
$E_{1}=188 {MeV}=188 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13} {~J}$
Energy released in the fission of $235{\text{ }}gm$ of $U^{235}$
$[\because$ Number of atoms is $235{\text{ }}gm$ of
${ }^{235} U=N=$ Avogadro number]
$=N \times 188 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}$
$=6.02 \times 10^{23} \times 188 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13} J$
$=1810.8 \times 10^{10}=1810 \times 10^{12} J$
$=18.10 \times 10^{12} J$
So, the correct option is (4).

question: The ratio of the relative strengths of strong and weak nuclear forces is
explanation: Strong Nuclear force is the force acting inside nucleus (between nucleons) of an atom. It is strongest force in nature fundamentally. Weak nuclear force is the force responsible for changing of neutron to proton or vice versa via beta-decay. It is not strong as compared to strong nuclear force. The ratio of strong and weak nuclear force's relative strength is $10^{13}$. So, the correct option is (1).

question:
Match the following.
(Take the relative strength of the strongest fundamental forces in nature as one)
explanation:
A - Q : Strong nuclear forces are those forces which acts between inside of the nucleus of an atom. It is strongest fundamental force in nature. We have taken its strength as 1 according to question.
B - S : Weak nuclear forces are those forces which comes in play in conversion of neutron to proton or vice versa, through - $p$ - decay. It is $\dfrac{1}{10^{13}}$ in strength in comparison with strong nuclear force.
C - P Electromagnetic forces act between the charge particles. It's strength with strong nuclear force is $\dfrac{1}{100}$ times.
D - T : Gravitational forces acts between bodies with masses. It is always attractive in nature. Its relative-strength to strong nuclear force is $\dfrac{1}{10^{39}}$ times. Hence, (A - Q, B - S, C - P, D - T) is correct order So, the correct option is (3).

explanation: Heavy water chemically it is written as ${D}_{2} {O}$ (also called as deuterium oxide) act as moderator in a nuclear reaction because it slows down the neutrons produced during fission process. Heavy water is an excellent moderator because it can work at high temperature and has low absorption cross-section for neutrons. Thus, option (4) is the correct option.

question: Energy released in the fission of a single uranium nucleus is  $200{\text{ }}MeV.$  Then the number of fissions per second to produce  $5{\text{ }}mW$  power is
explanation:
Given, Energy released in the fission of single uranium nucleus $=200 {MeV}$
Power $=5 {~mW}$
Using the formula $P=n E$
[where $n$ is number of fission reaction per sec and $E=$ energy released per fission]
$\therefore \quad n = \frac{P}{E} = \frac{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{{200 \times {{10}^6} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}$ $ = 1.56 \times {10^8}{S^{ - 1}}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: What will be the energy released in joule, in the process of fission by 1 mg of ${ }_{92}^{240} U$. Assume energy release per fission is  $200{\text{ }}MeV.$  [use Avogadro's number as $6 \times 10^{23}$ and     $1eV = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}\,J\,\,]$
explanation:
Total energy released $=$ Total number of fission $\times$ Energy released per fission
$=m \times \dfrac{N}{M} \times 200 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} J$
Here $M=$ Mass number and $N=$ Avogadro number.
$=1 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{6 \times 10^{23}}{240} \times 200 \times 10^{6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$
$=8 \times 10^{7} J$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: The physics behind fusion test reactor is
explanation: In case of nuclear fusion reaction, light atomic nuclei such as hydrogen isotopes fuses with each other and produces the helium nuclei due to the property of magnetic confinement. Because magnetic confinement is the phenomenon in which particle and energy of hot plasma are held together by using very strong magnetic field, that confines the movement of deuterium-tritium plasma and prevents them from coming in contact with reactor wall, that may dissipate heat of nuclei and slow down the reaction. So, the correct option is (3).

Motion of charged particles in electromagnetic fields

question: If  $5{\text{ }}mg$   of ${ }^{235} {U}$ is completely destroyed in an atom bomb, the approximate total energy released is (given that, the energy released per fission is $200{\text{ }}MeV)$

$E=\Delta m c^{2}=5 \times 10^{-6} \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2}=0.4 \times 10^{8} J$

question: Which one of the following statements is correct?
explanation: The nucleons inside the nucleus are very strongly bound and energies of few  $MeV$  are needed to separate a nucleon from the nucleus. Hence, a minimum energy is required to split the protons and neutrons and that energy is known as the binding energy of the nucleus. If, there are $Z$ number of protons and $N$ number of neutrons and mass of the nucleus is $M(A, Z)$, then binding energy $E_{B}=Z m_{p} c^{2}+N m_{n} c^{2}-M(A, Z) c^{2}>0$ where $m_{p}$ and $m_{n}$ are the masses of protons and neutrons, respectively. Hence, we can conclude that, the mass of the nucleus must be less than the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons. So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: The mass of the nucleus must be less than the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons.
The mass of the nucleus must be equal to the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons.
The mass of the nucleus must be greater than the sum of the masses of the constituent neutrons and protons.
The mass of the nucleus must be equal to only the masses of the constituent neutrons or protons .

subject: Physics
topic: Mass Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy

question:
What is the binding energy of ${ }_{14}^{29} {Si}$, whose atomic mass is $28.976495 u$ ?
Mass of proton $=1.007276 u$
Mass of neutron $=1.008664 u$
(neglect the electron mass)
(assume, $1 u=931.5 {MeV}$)
explanation:
Given, mass of ${ }_{14}^{29} {Si}=28.976495 u$
Now, mass of 14 protons
$=14 \times$ mass of 1 proton
$=14 \times 1.007276=14.101864 u$
Also, mass of $29-14=15$ neutrons $=15 \times$ mass of $I$ neutron
$=15 \times 1.008664=15.12996 u$
Total mass of constituents of Si atom
$=14.101864 u+15.12996 u=29.231824 u$
So, mass defect $\Delta M=$ mass of constituents $ - $ mass of atom
$=29.231824 u-28.976495 u=0.255329 u$
Binding energy $=$ Energy equivalent of mass defect $=\Delta M \times 931.5 {MeV}$
$=0.255329 \times 931.5=237.84 {MeV}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics
topic: Mass Energy and Nuclear Binding Energy

question: The binding energy ($B E$) per nucleon for an element is  $7.14{\text{ }}MeV.$  If the $B E$ of element is  $28.6{\text{ }}MeV,$   then the number of nucleons in the element is
explanation:
Given, binding energy ( $B E$ ) per nucleon $ = 7.14MeV$
BE of element $=28.6 {MeV}$
We know that,
Number of nucleons $=\dfrac{B E \text { of element }}{B E \text { per nucleon }}$
$=\dfrac{28.6}{7.14}=4$
So, the correct option is (1)

question: In a nuclear fission reaction of an isotope of mass $M$. Three similar daughter nuclei of same mass are formed. The speed of a daughter nuclei in terms of mass defect $\Delta M$ will be
explanation:
As, $E = \Delta {\mkern 1mu} M{c^2}\quad \quad (1)$
As, after fission, 3 similar daughter nuclei of same mass are formed, their kinetic energy will be
$E = 3 \times \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{M}{3}} \right){v^2}\quad \quad (2)$
By comparing (1) and (2)
$\Delta M c^{2}=\dfrac{1}{2} M v^{2}$
${v^2} = \frac{{2\,\Delta \,M{c^2}}}{M} \Rightarrow v = \sqrt {\frac{{2\,\Delta \,M{c^2}}}{M}} $
$ \Rightarrow v = c\sqrt {\frac{{2\,\Delta \,M}}{M}} $
subject: Physics

question:
The explosive in a Hydrogen bomb is a mixture of $_1{H^2},\,{\,_1}{H^3}$ and ${ }_{3} L i^{6}$ in some condensed form.
The chain reaction is given by
${ }_{3} L i^{6}+{ }_{0} n^{1} \rightarrow{ }_{2} H e^{4}+{ }_{1} H^{3}$
${ }_{1} H^{2}+{ }_{1} H^{3} \rightarrow{ }_{2} H e^{4}+{ }_{0} n^{1}$. During the explosion the energy released is approximately
[Given : $M\left( {Li} \right) = 6.01690\,amu,$
$M\left( {_1{H^2}} \right) = 2.01471\,amu,$
$M\left( {_2H{e^4}} \right) = 4.00388\,amu,$
${\rm{and}}\,\,\,\,1\,amu = 931.5\,MeV$
explanation:
${\text{Adding}}\,\,\,\,\frac{\begin{gathered} _3L{i^6}{ + _0}{n^1}{ \to _2}H{e^4}{ + _1}{H^3} \hfill \\ _1{H^2}{ + _1}{H^3}{ \to _2}H{e^4}{ + _0}{n^1} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} }{{_3L{i^6}{ + _1}{H^2} \to 2\left( {_2H{e^4}} \right)}}$
Energy released in process,
$Q = \left( {{M_{{\text{reactants }}}} - {M_{{\text{products }}}}} \right) \times 931.5MeV$
$Q=\left[M\left({ }_{3} L i^{6}\right)+M\left({ }_{1} H^{2}\right)-2 \times M\left({ }_{2} H e^{4}\right)\right]$
$ \times \,\,931.5\,MeV$
$Q=[(6.01690+2.01471-2 \times 4.00388)]$
$ \times \,\,931.5\,MeV$
$Q = 22.216\,MeV \approx 22.22\,MeV$
subject: Physics

question: The energy released in the fusion of  $2\,kg$  of hydrogen deep in the sun is $E_{H}$ and the energy released in the fission of $2\,kg$ of  $^{235}U$  is $E_{U}$. The ratio $\dfrac{E_{H}}{E_{U}}$ is approximately  (Consider the fusion reaction as,  $4_1^1H + 2{e^ - } \to _2^4He + 2v + 6\gamma  + 26.7MeV$ energy released in the fission reaction of ${ }^{235} U$ is  $200\,MeV$ per fission nucleus and  ${N_A} = 6.023 \times {10^{23}}\,)$
explanation:
Energy released in each fussion is
$ = 26.7\,MeV$
No of atoms in $2\,kg$ is $=N_{A} \times \dfrac{m}{M}=N_{A} \times \dfrac{2000}{1}$
No of fusion is $=\dfrac{N_{A}}{4} \times 2000$
Total energy released is
${E_H} = \frac{{2000}}{4} \times {N_A} \times 26.7\,MeV$
Energy released by $2\,\,kg\,{U^{235}}$ is
$E_{U}=\dfrac{2000}{235} \times N_{A} \times 200$
So, $\dfrac{E_{H}}{E_{U}}=\dfrac{235 \times 6.675}{200} \approx 7.62$

question: A nucleus at rest disintegrates inte two smaller nuclei with their masses in the ratio of 2: 1. After disintegration they will move
explanation:
${m_1}:{m_2} = 2:1$
Using the conservation of momentum, $\overrightarrow{p_{i}}=\overrightarrow{p_{f}}$
$0 = {m_1}\overrightarrow {{v_1}} + {m_2}\overrightarrow {{v_2}} $
$ \Rightarrow {m_1}{{\vec v}_1} = - {m_2}\overrightarrow {{v_2}} $
$\frac{{{v_1}}}{{{v_2}}} = \left| { - \frac{{{m_2}}}{{{m_1}}}} \right| = \left| {\frac{1}{2}} \right| = 1:2$
Minus sign shows that they move in opposite directions.

options.text: in opposite directions with speed in the ratio of $1: 2$ respectively
in the same directions with same speed
in opposite directions with the same speed
in opposite directions with speed in the ratio of 2: 1 respectively

question: In the process of nuclear fission of 1 gram uranium, the mass lost is 0.92 milligram. The efficiency of power house run by the fission reactor is ${10 \%}$. To obtain 400-megawatt power from the power house, how much uranium  will be required per hour? ${\left(c=3 \times 10^{8} {~m} / {s}\right)}$
explanation:
Power to be obtained from power house $=400 {MW}$
In this case energy obtained per hour
$=400 {MW} \times 1$ hour $=\left(400 \times 10^{6} {~W}\right) \times 3600 {~s}=144 \times 10^{10} {~J}$
Here only $10 \%$ of output is utilized. In order to obtain $144 \times 10^{10}$ joule of useful energy.
The output energy from the power house.
$E=\dfrac{\left(144 \times 10^{10}\right) \times 100}{10}=144 \times 10^{11} {~J}$
Let this energy be obtained from a mass-loss of $\Delta m {~kg}$.
Hence $(\Delta m) c^{2}=144 \times 10^{11} {~J}$
or $\Delta m=\dfrac{144 \times 10^{11}}{\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2}}=16 \times 10^{-1} {~kg}=0.16 {gm}$
Since 0.92 milli gram $\left( { = 0.92 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}\,g} \right)$ mass is lost in 1 $g$ uranium,
hence for a mass loss of 0.16 $g$ the uranium required.
$\Delta m^{\prime}=\dfrac{1 \times 0.16}{0.92 \times 10^{-3}}=174 {gm}$
Thus, to run the power house, 174 $gm$ uranium is required per hour.
subject: Physics

question:
Find the minimum kinetic energy (in eV ) of an
${\alpha}$-particle to cause the reaction ${{ }^{14} {~N}(\alpha, p){ }^{17} {O}}$. The masses of ${{ }^{14} {~N},{ }^{4} {He},{ }^{1} {H}}$ and ${{ }^{17} {O}}$ are respectively $14.00307{\rm{ }}u,{\rm{ }}4.00260{\rm{ }}u,{\rm{ }}1.007.83{\rm{ }}u$ and $16.99913{\rm{ }}u.$
explanation:
Since the masses are given in atomic mass units, it is easiest to proceed by finding the mass difference between reactants and products in the same units and then multiplying by ${931.5 {MeV} / {u}}$. Thus, we have
$Q = (14.00307\,u + 4.00260\,u$
$ - 1.00783\,u - 16.99913\,u)\left( {931.5\frac{{MeV}}{u}} \right)$
$ = {\rm{ }} - 1.20\,MeV$
${Q}$ value is negative. It means reaction is endothermic
So, the minimum kinetic energy of ${\alpha}$-particle to initiate this reaction would be,
${K_{\min }} = |Q|\left( {\frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_N}}} + 1} \right)$
$ = (1.20)\left( {\frac{{4.00260}}{{14.00307}} + 1} \right) = 1.54\,MeV$
subject: Physics

question: The fission of $^{235}U$ can be triggered by the absorption of a slow neutron by a nucleus. Similarly a slow proton can also be used. This statement is
explanation: Because the neutron has no electric charge, it experiences no electric repulsion from a  ${U^{235}}$ nucleus. Hence, a slow moving neutron can approach and enter a ${U^{235}}$ nucleus, thereby providing the excitation needed to trigger fission. In contrast, a slow moving proton feels a strong repulsion from a ${U^{235}}$ nucleus. It never gets close to the nucleus, so it cannot trigger nuclear fission. Hence, given statement is incorrect.

${\text{Here}}\,,{\text{ }}\Sigma {B_r} = 3{E_b},\Sigma {B_p} = {E_a},Q = e$
$\therefore \quad e = 3{E_b} - {E_a} \Rightarrow {E_a} + e = 3{E_b}$

question:
Assertion: Heavy water is used to slow neutron in nuclear reactor.

Reason: It does not react with slow neutron and mass of deuterium is comparable to the neutron.
explanation: Heavy water $\left(\mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$ is used to slow down fast moving neutrons in nuclear reactor, hence it is called moderator. It does remove neutrons from the system by absorbing them. When fast moving neutrons are passed through heavy water $\left(\mathrm{D}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)$, then they make elastic collisions with protons or deuterium having mass comparable to neutrons, which have comparatively much smaller than velocities. In few interactions, the velocities of neutrons get interchanged with those of protons. Heavy water does not react with slow moving neutrons, $i.\,e.$  it slow down-fast moving neutrons.Hence, Assertion and Reason both are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.

question: $10^{14}$ fissions per second are taking place in a nuclear reactor having efficiency $40 \%$. The energy released per fission is $250\,MeV$. The power output of the reactor is
explanation:
Energy released per fission ${=250 {MeV}}$
Total energy released per sec
${\rm{ = }}250 \times {10^6} \times {10^{14}} \times \frac{{40}}{{100}}$
$ = {\rm{ }}100 \times {10^{20}} = {10^{22}}eV/sec$
Energy per second ${=10^{22} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} {~J} / {sec}}$
${=1.6 \times 10^{3} {~J} / {sec}}$
So, power output ${\quad=1600 {~W}}$

question:
${ }_{92}^{238} A \rightarrow{ }_{90}^{234} B+{ }_{2}^{4} D+Q$
In the given nuclear reaction, the approximate amount of energy released will be
[Given,mass of
$_{92}^{238}A = 238.05079 \times 931.5MeV/{c^2}$
mass of $_{90}^{234}B = 234.04363 \times 931.5MeV/{c^2},$
mass of $_2^4D = 4.00260 \times 931.5MeV/{c^2}$
explanation:
The energy released during the given reaction will be
$\Delta Q=\Delta m c^{2} \quad$ where $\Delta m=$ mass defect
$ = \left( {{m_A} - {m_B} - {m_D}} \right) \times 931.5\,MeV$
$=(238.05079-234.04363-4.00260) \times 931.5$
$ = 4.25\,MeV$

question: The atomic mass of ${ }_{6} C^{12}$ is $12.000000{\rm{ }}\,\,u$ and that of ${ }_{6} C^{13}$ is $13.003354{\rm{ }}\,\,u$. The required energy to remove a neutron from ${ }_{6} C^{13}$, if mass of neutron is $1.008665{\rm{ }}\,\,u$, will be :
explanation:
${ }_{6}^{13} C \rightarrow{ }_{6}^{12} C+{ }_{0}^{1} n$
$\Delta m = 12 + 1.008665 - 13.003354$
$\Delta m = 0.005311\,\,amu$
Using mass energy relation i.e.,
$u = \Delta m \times 931.5\,\,MeV\,\,\,\,$($\Delta m = $ mass defect $)$
$u=4.947$
$ \approx 4.95\,\,MeV$

question:
Assertion: Thermonuclear fusion reactions may become the source of unlimited power for the mankind.

Reason: A single fusion event involving isotopes of hydrogen produces more energy than energy from nuclear fission of a single uranium.
explanation: Percentage of mass converted in fusion is more $(0.4 \%)$ than in fission $(0.1 \%)$. In thermonuclear fusion, particles are heated to overcome coulomb repulsion, However, the energy released per fusion event is less than (of order $20\,MeV$) a uranium fission event $\left( {200\,MeV} \right)$. Thermo nuclear fusion is taking place in Sun (at temperatures of order of ${10^7}\,K$). On earth, in laboratories (like IPR in India, ITER in Europe), controlled fusion is being tried (not yet fulfilled). So correct option is (3).

$\begin{aligned}m_{f} & =2 \times 4.002603=8.005206 \\\Delta m & =m_{i}-m_{f} \\& =8.023829-8.005206=0.018623 u\end{aligned}$
The $Q$ - value is given by
$Q = (\Delta m){c^2} = 0.018623 \times 931.5$
$ = 17.35\,{\rm{MeV}}$.

$\Rightarrow Q=n E=\dfrac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{235} \times 200 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}$
$ = 8.202 \times {10^{10}}\,J.$

question: The binding energy per nucleon of dueterium and helium nuclei are $1.1\,MeV$ and $7\,MeV$ respectively. When two deuterium nuclei fuse to form a helium nucleus the energy released in the fusion is:

The binding energy per nucleon of a deuterium $ = 1.1\,MeV$.
$\therefore$ Total binding energy $ = 4 \times 1.1 = 4.4\,MeV$.
The binding energy per nucleon of a helium nuclei $ = 7\,MeV$.
$\therefore$ Total binding energy $ = 4 \times 7 = 28\,MeV$.
Hence, energy released.
$\Delta E = (28 - 4.4)\,MeV = 23.6\,MeV.$

question: A nuclear explosive is designed to deliver $1\,MW$ power in the form of heat energy. If the explosion is designed with nuclear fuel consisting of $U^{235}$ to run a reactor at this power level for one year, then the amount of fuel needed is (take, energy per fission is $200\,MeV$)
explanation:

$E = 200\,MeV = 200 \times 26 \times {10^{ - 13}}$
$ = 3.2 \times {10^{ - 11}}\,J$
Total energy required to run a $1\,MW$ reaction for one year $ = {10^6}\,J\,{S^{ - 1}} \times (365 \times 24 \times 60 \times 60)$
$ = 3.15 \times {10^{13}}\,J$
Since, 1 fission (atom of $U^{235}$ ) produces
$3.2 \times 10^{-11} J$ of energy.
So, total number of $U^{235}$ atoms required is
$\dfrac{3.15 \times 10^{13}}{3.2 \times 10^{-11}}=9.84 \times 10^{23}$.
$\because$ Mass of $U^{235}$ containing $9.84 \times 10^{23}$ atom,
$M=\dfrac{235}{6.02 \times 10^{23}}=9.84 \times 10^{23}$
$ = 384\,g = 0.384\,kg$
subject: Physics

question:
Assertion: The binding energy per nucleon, for nuclei with atomic mass number $A>100$, decrease with $A$.

Reason: The nuclear forces are weak for heavier nuclei.
explanation:
It is a well-established fact that the binding energy per nucleon decreases for heavy nuclei $(\mathrm{A}>100)$. This phenomenon is primarily due to the increasing influence of the Coulomb repulsion between protons as the number of protons in the nucleus increases. The Coulomb repulsion counteracts the attractive nuclear force, leading to a decrease in the binding energy per nucleon. The nuclear forces, often referred to as the strong nuclear force, are responsible for holding the nucleus together. The strong nuclear force is not weak for heavier nuclei; it is the dominant force that overcomes the electrostatic repulsion between protons and maintains nuclear stability. The decrease in binding energy per nucleon for heavy nuclei is primarily due to the increasing Coulomb repulsion between protons, not the weakness of the nuclear forces.
The binding energy per nucleon decreases in heavy nuclei due to the Coulomb repulsion, not the weakness of the nuclear forces. So, the assertion is correct, but the reason provided is incorrect. So option (3) is correct

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

subject: Physics

question:

$B \to 2A$

question:
A star initially has ${10^{40}}$ deuterons. It produces energy via the processes
$_1{H^2}{ + _1}{H^2}{ \to _1}{H^3} + p$
$_1{H^2}{ + _1}{H^3}{ \to _2}H{e^4} + n$
The masses of the nuclei are as follows:
$M({H^2}) = 2.014\,amu;M(p) = 1.007\,amu;$
$M(n) = 1.008\,amu;M(H{e^4}) = 4.001\,amu$
If the average power radiated by the star is ${10^{16}}W,$ the deuteron supply of the star is exhausted in a time of the order of
explanation:
The net reaction is
${3_1}{H^2} \to {}_2H{e^4} + p + n$
Mass defect $ = 3 \times 2.014 - 4.001 - 1.007 - 1.008$
$Q = 0.026\,amu = 0.026 \times 931 \times {10^6} \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J$
$ = 3.87 \times {10^{ - 12}}J$
Power radiated by the star $ = {10^{16}}W$
A single reaction consumes 3-deuterons.
So 3- deuterons produce $ - 3.87 \times {10^{ - 12}}J$
1-deuteron produce $ - 1.29 \times {10^{ - 12}}J$
Star produces $ - {10^{16}}J$ in $1s$
No of deuterons consumed per second
$\frac{{{{10}^{16}}}}{{1.29 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}} = \frac{1}{{1.29}} \times {10^{28}}$
Deuteron supply exhausts in
$\frac{{{{10}^{40}}}}{{0.775 \times {{10}^{28}}}} = 1.29 \times {10^{12}}\,{\text{s}}$
subject: Physics

question: Assume that a neutron breaks into a proton and 	an electron. The energy absorbed during this process is: (mass of neutron  	$ = 1.6725 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg,$ mass of proton $ = 1.6725 \times {10^{ - 27}}kg,$ mass of electron $ = 9 \times {10^{ - 31}}kg$).
explanation:
${}_0^1n \to {}_1^1P{ + _{ - 1}}{e^0} + \bar v + Q$
The mass defect during the process
$\Delta m = {m_n} - {m_H} - {m_e} = 1.6725 \times {10^{27}}$
$ - (1.6725 \times {10^{ - 27}} + 9 \times {10^{ - 31}}kg)$
$ = - 9 \times {10^{ - 31}}kg$
Here $\Delta m$ is $( - )ve$ so the reaction absorbs energy.The energy absorbed during the process
$E = \Delta m{c^2}$
$E = 9 \times {10^{ - 31}} \times 9 \times {10^{16}} = 81 \times {10^{ - 15}}{\text{joules}}$
$E = \frac{{81 \times {{10}^{ - 15}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}} = 0.511{\mkern 1mu} $ $Mev$
The correct answer is $ - 0.511\,MeV$

explanation: Statement  A  is false because, unlike Coulomb's law or the Newton's law of gravitation, there is no simple mathematical form of the nuclear force. Statement B is also false; while the nuclear force does depend on the distance between nucleons, it is not a central force like gravity or electrostatic force, as it also involves spin and other quantum properties of nucleons. Correct option is (4)

explanation: Statement A is true as the nuclear force is approximately the same regardless of the combinations of nucleons (neutron-neutron, proton-neutron, or proton-proton). Statement B is false because the nuclear force does not depend on the electric charge of the nucleons; it acts the same way between protons and neutrons despite the protons being positively charged and neutrons being neutral. explanation: Statement  A  is true because the nuclear force is much stronger than both the Coulomb and gravitational forces, which is necessary to bind nucleons within the nucleus. Statement B is also true because the short-range nature of the nuclear force leads to the saturation of forces in the nucleus, contributing to the constancy of the binding energy per nucleon. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Statement A and Statement B are true.

Both Statement A and Statement B are false.

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark correct option given below:

Assertion: The nuclear force between two nucleons is attractive for distances larger than about 0.8 femtometres and repulsive for shorter distances.

Reason: This behaviour of nuclear force ensures that nucleons can be close enough to be bound within the nucleus without collapsing into each other.
explanation: The nuclear force exhibits a short-range nature, being attractive at distances greater than about 0.8 femtometres and becoming repulsive at shorter distances. This characteristic allows nucleons to be bound within the nucleus with sufficient strength while preventing them from collapsing into a more compact space due to the repulsive force that arises at very short distances. The attractive nuclear force is essential for overcoming the electrostatic repulsive forces between the positively charged protons in the nucleus, enabling the nucleus to exist as a stable, bound system. The repulsive component at very short distances prevents the nucleons from being compressed into an infinitely small point, which would not be physically meaningful, hence contributing to the stability and finite size of the nucleus. Correct option is (1)

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true and    Reason is the correct explanation of the  Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but  Reason is not the correct explanation of the    Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.

subject: Physics

Gravitational force is the weakest fundamental force in the nature. It acts between bodies with masses.
Weak nuclear force is the force which allows proton to turn into neutron and vice-versa through beta decay. The ratio of strength of weak nuclear force to gravitational force is $10^{26}$.
So, the correct option is (4).

Thus, the correct order of strength is $F_{1} < F_{2} < F_{3}$
So, the correct option is (2).

question: The ratio of the relative strengths of strong and weak nuclear forces is
explanation: Strong Nuclear force is the force acting inside nucleus (between nucleons) of an atom. It is strongest force in nature fundamentally. The ratio of strong and weak nuclear force's relative strength is $10^{13}$. So, the correct option is (1).

B - S : Weak nuclear forces are those forces which comes in play in conversion of neutron to proton or vice versa, through - $p$ - decay. It is $\dfrac{1}{10^{13}}$ in strength in comparison with strong nuclear force.
C - P Electromagnetic forces act between the charge particles. It's strength with strong nuclear force is $\dfrac{1}{100}$ times.
D - T : Gravitational forces acts between bodies with masses. It is always attractive in nature. Its relative-strength to strong nuclear force is $\dfrac{1}{10^{39}}$ times. Hence, (A - Q, B - S, C - P, D - T) is correct order So, the correct option is (3).

question: If the ratio of the radii of nuclei ${ }_{52} X^{A}$ and ${ }_{13} A l^{27}$ is $5: 3$, then the number of neutrons in the nucleus $X$ is
explanation:
For two nuclei ${ }_{52} X^{A}$ and $_{13}A{l^{27}},$ $R_{1}: R_{2}=5: 3$
Radius of nucleus, $R=R_{0} A^{1 / 3}$ where $A$ is the mass number.
$\therefore \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}\right)^{1 / 3}=\left(\dfrac{A}{27}\right)^{1 / 3}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{A}{27}=\left(\dfrac{5}{3}\right)^{3}=\dfrac{125}{27} \therefore A=125$
$\therefore \quad$ Number of neutrons in nucleus ${ }_{52} X^{125}$ is $n=A-Z=125-52=73$

question: The mass number of an iodine nucleus and a polonium nucleus is 125 and 216,   respectively. The ratio of radius of iodine nucleus to that of polonium nucleus is
explanation:
Given, mass number of iodine $I$ and polonium $P$ are $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$ respectively.
Given $A_{1}=125 \Rightarrow A_{2}=216$
Let their radii be $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$.
As we know that, $R=R_{0} A^{1 / 3}$ where, $R=$ radius of atom, $A=$ mass number.
$\therefore \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}\right)^{1 / 3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{125}{216}\right)^{1 / 3}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{5^{3}}{6^{3}}\right)^{1 / 3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\dfrac{5}{6}$
Hence, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: If $S$ is the surface area of a nucleus of mass number $A$, then
explanation:
Mass number of nucleus $=A$
Surface area of nucleus $=S$
Using the relation, $R=R_{0}(A)^{1 / 3}$,
Radius $\propto A^{1 / 3}$
Nucleus is spherical in shape, so surface area $=4 \pi R^{2}$
or $S=4 \pi\left(R_{0} A^{1 / 3}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow S \propto A^{2 / 3}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: The radius of a nucleus of mass number 27 is $R$. Which of the following is true about a nucleus whose radius is $2 R$ ?
explanation:
We know that, radius of a nucleus of mass number $A$ is given as $R \propto A^{1 / 3}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}=\left(\dfrac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}\right)^{1 / 3} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2 R}{R}=\left(\dfrac{A_{2}}{27}\right)^{1 / 3}$
$\Rightarrow 2=\left(\dfrac{A_{2}}{27}\right)^{1 / 3} \Rightarrow A_{2}=8 \times 27=216$
Thus, we see that nuclei having mass number 216 is heavier nuclei. Binding energy per nucleon is smaller for such heavier nuclei. Thus, it is likely to undergo fission reaction. So, the correct option is (3).

options.text: It is likely to undergo fission reaction
It is likely to undergo fusion reaction

subject: Physics

question: As the mass number $A$ increases, which of the following quantities related to a nucleus does not change?
explanation:
Let $A$ be the mass number and $R$ be the radius of a nucleus. If $m$ be the average mass of a nucleon, then mass of nucleus, $M=m A$
Volume of nucleus, $V=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}$
$\therefore$ Nuclear density,
$\rho=\dfrac{\text { Mass of nucleus }(M)}{\text { Volume }(V)}$
$ = \frac{{mA}}{{\frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}}} = \frac{{3mA}}{{4\pi {R^3}}}$ $ = \frac{{3mA}}{{4\pi {{\left( {{R_0}{A^{\frac{1}{3}}}} \right)}^3}}}\quad \left( {\because R = {R_0}{R^{1/3}}} \right)$
$\therefore \rho=\dfrac{3 m A}{4 \pi R_{0}^{3} A}=\dfrac{3 m}{4 \pi R_{0}^{3}}$
Hence, nuclear density does not depend on mass number.
Therefore, with increase of mass number $A$, nuclear density does not change.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: The radius and mass number of nucleus 1 is $R_{1}$ and $A_{1}$, respectively. The radius and mass number of nucleus 2 is $R_{2}$ and $A_{2}$, respectively. If $A_{2}$ is larger than $A_{1}$ by $2 \%$, then $R_{2}$ is larger than $R_{1}$ by
explanation:
We know that, radius of nucleus in terms of mass number $(A)$ is given as
$R=R_{0}\left(A^{1 / 3}\right)$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{R_2}}}{{{R_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_2}}}{{{A_1}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
But, $A_{2}=A_{1}+2 \%$ of $A_{1}$
$=A_{1}+\dfrac{2}{100} A_{1}=\dfrac{102}{100} A_{1}=1.02 A_{1}$
From Eq (1), we get
$\frac{{{R_2}}}{{{R_1}}} = {\left( {\frac{{1.02{A_1}}}{{{A_1}}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{3}}} = {(1.02)^{1/3}}$ $ \Rightarrow {R_2} = {R_1}{(1.02)^{1/3}}$
$=R_{1}(1+0.02)^{1 / 3}=R_{1}\left(1+\dfrac{1}{3} \times 0.02\right)$
$R_{2}=1.0067 R_{1}=R_{1}+0.0067 R_{1}$
$=R_{1}+0.67 \%$ of $R_{1}=R_{1}+\dfrac{2}{3} \%$ of $R_{1}$
Hence, $R_{2}$ is larger than $R_{1}$ by $\dfrac{2}{3} \%$.
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a nucleus ${ }_{30}^{60} X$. Its approximate density is (take, $1 {amu}=1.6 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}, R_{0}=1.2 \times 10^{-15} {~m}$)
explanation:
Given, 1 amu $=1.6 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}$ and
$R_{0}=1.2 \times 10^{-15} {~m}$
Since, nuclear density is independent of mass number, the nuclear density of nucleus ${ }_{30}^{60} {X}$ is given as:
$\rho = \frac{{3m}}{{4\pi R_0^3}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
where, $m$ is average mass of nucleon.
Average mass of nucleon $m=\dfrac{m_{n}+m_{p}}{2}$
$=\dfrac{1.0073+1.0086}{2} {amu}=1.00795 {amu}$
$=1.00795 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}$
$\Rightarrow \quad m=1.61272 \times 10^{-27} {~kg}$
$\therefore$ Putting the values in Eq. (1), we get
$\rho=\dfrac{3 \times 1.61272 \times 10^{-27}}{4 \times 3.14 \times\left(1.2 \times 10^{-15}\right)^{3}} {~kg}-{m}^{-3}$
$=2.2 \times 10^{17} {~kg} {~m}^{-3}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: What is the mass number of the nucleus having radius equal to $1 / 3$ of that of ${ }^{189} {Os}$ ?
explanation:
Radius of, nucleus is $R=R_{0}(A)^{1 / 3}$
For $O s$ where $R_{0}$ is a constant and $A$ is mass number.
$R=R_{0}(189)^{1 / 3}$
Radius of given nucleus,
$R^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{3} R \Rightarrow R^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{3} R_{0}(189)^{1 / 3}$
But, $R^{\prime}=R_{0}\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{1 / 3}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{3} R_{0}(189)^{1 / 3}=R_{0}(A)^{1 / 3}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{3}(189)^{1 / 3}=\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{1 / 3}$
Cubing both side, we get
$A^{\prime}=\dfrac{1}{3^{3}} \cdot 189=\dfrac{189}{27}=7$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Estimate the approximate volume of aluminium nucleus $(A=27)$, use $\binom{R_{0} \simeq 1.0 \times 10^{-15} {~m}}{\pi \simeq 3}$
explanation:
Given, for $A l$ nucleus
Mass number, $A = 27,$
${R_0} = 1 \times {10^{ - 15}}m$ and $\pi=3$
We know that, radius of nucleus is given as
$R=R_{0} A^{1 / 3}$
$=1 \times 10^{-15} \times(27)^{1 / 3}=3 \times 10^{-15} {~m}$
Volume of aluminium ( $A l$ ) nucleus,
$V=\dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}=\dfrac{4}{3} \times \pi \times\left(3 \times 10^{-15}\right)^{3} {~m}^{3}$
$=\dfrac{4}{3} \times 3 \times 27 \times 10^{-45}=108 \times 10^{-45} {~m}^{3}$
$=1.08 \times 10^{-13} \times\left(10^{-10}\right)^{3} {~m}^{3}$
$ = 1.08 \times {10^{ - 13}} \times (1$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $)$^3$ $ \simeq 1 \times {10^{ - 13}}$$ \mathop A^{~~\circ} $$^3$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: The mass number and the volume of a nucleus is $M$ and $V$, respectively. If the mass number is increased to $2 M$, then the volume is changed to
explanation:
Given, mass number, $M_{1}=M$
Initial volume, $V_{1}=V$
Final mass number, $M_{2}=2 M$
As we know that, $R=R_{0} A^{1 / 3}$
$ \Rightarrow R \propto {A^{1/3}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
where, $R=$ radius of nucleus, $R_{0}=$ a constant and $A=$ mass number.
Since, volume $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {R^3}\quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
$ \Rightarrow \quad R \propto {(V)^{1/3}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
Now, from Eqs. (1) and (2), we get
$R \propto A^{1 / 3} \propto V^{1 / 3}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{M_{1}}{M_{2}}=\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}$
$\binom{\because A_{1}=M_{1}}{\text { and } A_{2}=M_{2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{M}{2 M}=\dfrac{V}{V_{2}} \Rightarrow V_{2}=2 V$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question:
The particle $A$ is converted into $C$ via following reaction
$A \to B{ + _2}H{e^4}$
$B \rightarrow C+2 e^{-}$
Then
explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& { }_{y} A^{x} \rightarrow_{y-2} B^{x-4}+{ }_{2} H e^{4} \\& { }_{y-2} B^{x-4} \rightarrow_{y} C^{x-4}+2_{-1} e^{0}\end{aligned}$
Hence, we observe that ${A}$ and ${C}$ are isotopes as their atomic numbers are same but mass numbers are different.

question:
Assertion: For the scattering of $\alpha$-particles at large angles, only the nucleus of the atom is responsible.

Reason: Nucleus is very heavy in comparison to electrons.
explanation: We know that electrons are considerably lighter than $\alpha$-particles. Therefore, according to the law of conservation of momentum, electrons cannot scatter $\alpha$-particles at large angles. But, the mass of the atomic nucleus is comparable to that of the $\alpha$-particle, which is why the nucleus alone is accountable for the scattering of $\alpha$-particles. So correct option is (1).

question: The radius of germanium $(Ge)$ nuclide is measured to be twice the radius of ${}_4B{e^9}$. The number of nucelons in $Ge$ are

$ \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^3} = \frac{9}{{{A_2}}} \Rightarrow {A_2} = 72$
Thus, in germanium $(Ge)$ nucleus 72 nucleons are present.

question: Atomic weight of  Boron is $10.81$ and it has two isotopes  ${}_5{B^{10}}$ and ${}_5{B^{11}}.$ Then the ratio of  isotopes in nature would be
explanation:
The number of ${}^{10}B$ type atoms $ = x$
And the number of ${}^{11}B$ type atoms $ = y$
Weight of ${}^{10}B$ type atoms $ = 10x$
Weight of ${}^{11}B$ type atoms $ = 11y$
Total number of atoms $ = x + y$
$\therefore $ Atomic weight $ = \frac{{10x + 11y}}{{x + y}} = 10.81$
$ \Rightarrow 10x + 11y = 10.81x + 10.81y$
$ \Rightarrow 0.81x = 0.19y \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y} = \frac{{19}}{{81}}$
subject: Physics

question: The end product of decay  $_{90}T{h^{232}}$  is  $_{82}\,P{b^{203}}.$  The number of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ particles emitted are respectively
explanation:
$_{90}T{h^{232}}{ \to _{82}}\,P{b^{208}}$
Change in atomic mass $=232-208=24$
Number of $\alpha$-particles emitted $=24 / 4=6$
Atomic number will decrease by $6 \times 2=12$
Atomic number will be increase by 4 due to emission $4 \beta$ particles.
So, net decrease in atomic number $=12-4=8$
subject: Physics

question: A nucleus of mass 20$u$ emits a $Y$ photon of energy 6 $MeV$ . If the emission is assumed to occur when nucleus is free and at rest, then the nucleus will have kinetic energy nearest to ( take$1u = 1.6 \times {10^{ - 27}}\,kg$ )
explanation:
Mass of nucleus, $M = 20u$
${E_1} = 6MeV = 6 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 13}}J$
Using momentum conservation principle
$0=M v+\dfrac{E_{\gamma}}{c}$
$\therefore v=-\dfrac{E_{\gamma}}{M c}=-\dfrac{6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13}}{20 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}$
$ = - {10^5}m{s^{ - 1}}$
$K.E$ of nucleus,
$K = \frac{1}{2}M{v^2}$
$=\dfrac{1}{2} \times 20 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times\left(10^{5}\right)^{2}=16 \times 10^{-17} J$
$ = \frac{{16 \times {{10}^{ - 17}}}}{{1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 16}}}}keV = 1keV$
subject: Physics

question: The half-life of a radioactive substance is 12 minutes. The time gap between $28 \%$ decay and $82 \%$ decay of the radioactive substance is
explanation:
After $28 \%$ decay number of active nuclei
$=N_{0}-\dfrac{N_{0} \times 28}{100}=\dfrac{72 N_{0}}{100}$
After $82 \%$ decay number of active nuclei
$=N_{0}-\dfrac{N_{0} \times 82}{100}=\dfrac{18 N_{0}}{100}$
Half life $T=12$ minutes
$\therefore \dfrac{72 N_{0}}{100}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t_{1}}{T}}$ and $\dfrac{18 N_{0}}{100}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t_{2}}{T}}$
$\therefore \dfrac{72}{18}=(2)^{\frac{t_{2}-t_{1}}{T}} \Rightarrow(2)^{2}=(2)^{\frac{\Delta t}{12}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\Delta t}{12}=2$
$\Rightarrow \Delta t=24 \mathrm{~min}$
subject: Physics

question: The half lives of two radioactive materials $A$ and $B$ are respectively $T$ and $2\,T$. If the ratio of the initial masses of the materials $A$ and $B$ is, $8: 1$ then the time after which the ratio of the masses of the materials $A$ and $B$ becomes 4:1 is
explanation:
From the law of radioactive decay.
$M=M_{0}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{t_{1 / 2}}$
Here, $M_{o}=$ initial mass
$M=$ mass left after time $t.$ ${t_{1/2}} = $ half life.
$\therefore \dfrac{M_{1}}{M_{2}}=\dfrac{M_{0}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_{1}}}}{M_{0}^{\prime}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_{2}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{4}{1} = \frac{8}{1}{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^{\frac{t}{T} - \frac{t}{{2T}}}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} = {\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^{\frac{t}{{2T}}}}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{t}{{2T}} = 1 \Rightarrow t = 2T$
subject: Physics

question: A $_{92}{U^{238}}$ nucleus decays to a  $_{82}\,P{b^{214}}$  nucleus. The number of $\alpha$ and $\beta^{-}$particles emitted are
explanation:

Number of $\beta$-particles $=82-(92-6 \times 2)=2$

question: After the decay of a single ' $\beta$ ' particle, the parent and daughter nuclei are
explanation:

question: Half-life periods of two nuclei $A$ and $B$ are $T$ and $2\,T$ respectively. Initially $A$ and $B$ have same number of nuclei. After a time of $4\,T$, the ratio of the remaining number of nuclei of $A$ and $B$ is
explanation:
For nucleus $A, n_{1}=\dfrac{t}{t_{1 / 2}}=\dfrac{4 T}{T}=4$
For nucleus $B, n_{2}=\dfrac{4 T}{2 T}=2$
$\therefore \quad$ Remaining number of active nuclei,
$N=N_{o}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$
$\therefore \dfrac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n_{1}-n_{2}}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{4-2}=1: 4$
subject: Physics

question: The nucleus ${ }_{50}^{120} X$ undergoes the series of reactions given below :    ${ }_{Z}^{A} X \xrightarrow{\alpha-\text { decay }} P \xrightarrow{\beta^{-} \text {-decay }} Q \xrightarrow{\alpha-\text { decay }} R$       The number of neutrons in the nucleus $R$ is
explanation:
The given nuclear reaction is shown as ${}_Z^AX\xrightarrow{{\alpha - {\text{ decay }}}}_{Z - 2}^{A - 4}P\xrightarrow{{{\beta ^ - } - {\text{decay }}}}_{Z - 1}^{A - 4}$ $Q\xrightarrow{{\alpha - {\text{ decay }}}}_{z - 3}^{A - 8}R$
No. of neutrons in the nucleus $R$
$ = (A - 8) - (Z - 3)$
$ = A - 8 - Z + 3$
$ = A - Z - 5$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: $\alpha$-decay of a parent nucleus $X$ results in a daughter nucleus $Y$. If $m_{x}, m_{y}$ and $m_{\alpha}$ are the masses of the parent nucleus, the daughter nucleus and the $\alpha$-particles respectively, then the net kinetic energy gained in the process is
explanation:
According to given situation,
$X \rightarrow Y+\alpha$
$\therefore$ Mass defect, $\Delta m=m_{x}-m_{y}-m_{\alpha}$
Net kinetic energy gained in the process $=$ energy released in nuclear reaction $=\Delta m \times c^{2}$ So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: The half-life period of element $X$ is same as the mean life time of element $Y$. Assume initially $X$ and $Y$ have same number of atoms. Then
explanation:
Given, Half life of $X=$ mean life of $Y$
Initially both have same number of atoms
Half life is given by, (for $X$ )
$t_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{x}}$
(1) Mean life for $Y$
$t = \frac{1}{{{\lambda _y}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
as $t_{1 / 2}$ of $X=t$ of $Y$
$\dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{x}}=\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{y}} \Rightarrow(0.6393) \lambda_{y}=\lambda_{x}$
$\therefore \lambda_{y}>\lambda_{x}$
Higher is the decay constant, higher is the rate of decay. Hence $Y$ will decay faster than $X$
So, the correct option is (3).

options.text: Initially $X$ and $Y$ have same decay rates
Always $X$ and $Y$ decay at same rate

subject: Physics

question: Half-life of a radioactive substance $A$ is two times the half-life of another radioactive substance $B$. Initially the number of nuclei of $A$ and $B$ are $N_{A}$ and $N_{B}$ respectively. After three half-lives of $A$, the number of nuclei of both are equal. Then $N_{A} / N_{B}$ is
explanation:
Given, Substance $A^{\prime} s$ half - life $=2 \times$ Substance' $B^{\prime} s$ half life.
Initial number of active nuclei of $A=N_{A}$
Initial number of active nuclei of $B=N_{B}$
Number of substance reaction per after $n$ half lives is,
$N=N_{0}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$
Now, $n=3$ for substance $A$ and $n=6$ for $B$
(It has half life is equal to $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of $A$ )
Remaining number of active nuclei of
$A=N_{A}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{3}$
Remaining number of active nuclei of
$B=N_{B}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{6}$
$N_{A}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{3}=N_{B}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{6}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{N_{A}}{N_{B}}=\dfrac{1}{8}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: Which of the following interaction is responsible for beta decay?
explanation:

question: A radioactive source has a half-life of $6 h$. A freshly prepared sample of the same exhibits radioactivity 32 times the permissible safe value. The minimum time after which it would be possible to work safely with the source is
explanation:

So, if $s$ be the permissible safe radioactive of source, then following cycle will be followed, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952833/preptracker/questions/q_111996_explanation_1764952833511.png Hence, sample becomes safe in 5 half-lives. i. e. , $T=5 \times 6=30 {~h}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: Alpha rays emitted from a radioactive substance are
explanation: $\alpha$ - rays are a stream of particles consisting of 2 neutrons and 2 protons. Hence, a $\alpha$ particle is a doubly ionised helium - atom. So, the correct option is (2)

question: The half-life of a radioactive sample is $5 s$. If the initial mass of the sample is  $60{\text{ }}g,$  then the time required to reduce the sample to  $7.5{\text{ }}g$  is
explanation:
Decay scheme of given sample is as shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958695/preptracker/questions/q_111994_explanation_1764958694651.png So, time required by the sample to be reduced to $7.5{\text{ }}g$ is
$\Delta t=3 \times T_{1 / 2}=3 \times 5=15 s$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: A radioactive element which can decay by two processes, has half-life $t_{1}$ for first process and half-life $t_{2}$ for second process. Let $\langle t\rangle$ be the effective average-life of this element. Which of the following is correct?
explanation:
Radioactive decay rate is directly proportional to number of nucleus present at any instant,
$\frac{{dN}}{{dt}} = - \lambda N\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
Here, $\lambda$ is a constant related to half-life $\left(\lambda=\dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1 / 2}}\right)$, where, $T_{1 / 2}$ is half-life time.
In given case, total rate of decay is
$\frac{{dN}}{{dt}} = - \left( {{\lambda _1} + {\lambda _2}} \right)N$ $ = - {\lambda _{{\text{effective}}}} \times N\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
Here, $\lambda_{1}=\dfrac{\ln 2}{t_{1}}$ and $\lambda_{2}=\dfrac{\ln 2}{t_{2}}$
Similarly, $\lambda_{\text {effective }}=\dfrac{\ln 2}{t}$ where $t=$ effective half life
Substituting these values in Eq. (2), we have
$\left(\dfrac{\ln 2}{t_{1}}+\dfrac{\ln 2}{t_{2}}\right) N=\dfrac{\ln 2}{t} N$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{t}=\dfrac{1}{t_{1}}+\dfrac{1}{t_{2}}=\dfrac{t_{1}+t_{2}}{t_{1} t_{2}} \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{t_{1} t_{2}}{t_{1}+t_{2}}$
Now $\langle t\rangle=\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{e f f}}=\dfrac{t}{\ln 2}=\dfrac{t}{0.693}$
$\Rightarrow < t \gg t$
So, the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: Half-life of radioactive sample is $24 h$. If a newly prepared radioactive sample shows 4 times the allowed and safe value of radio activity, the minimum time after which one can work safely with the source is
explanation:
Newly prepared radioactive sample shows 4 times the allowed and safe value of radioactivity, so on reaching to the allowed and safe value, its radioactivity will be reduced to ${\left( {\frac{1}{4}} \right)^{{\text{th}}}}$ of the current value,
$\because \quad A_{t}=\dfrac{A_{0}}{(2)^{t / t_{1 / 2}}}$
Here, $A_{0}=$ current activity, $A_{t}=$ final activity
$=\dfrac{A}{4}, t=$ time-taken, $t_{1 / 2}=$ half-life $=24 {~h}$
So, on putting the values
$\begin{aligned}\dfrac{A_{0}}{4} & =\dfrac{A_{0}}{(2)^{1 / t_{1 / 2}}} \Rightarrow(2)^{t / t_{1 / 2}}=4 \Rightarrow(2)^{t / 24}=(2)^{2} \\& \Rightarrow \dfrac{t}{24}=2 \Rightarrow t=48 {~h}\end{aligned}$
So, the correct option is (1)
subject: Physics

question: The half - life of a radioactive isotope is  $30{\text{ }}h.$  How long will it take to get reduced to $12.5 \%$ of its initial amount?
explanation:
Half-life of radioactive isotope,
$T_{1 / 2}=30 {~h}$
If $N_{0}$ be the initial amount of radioactive isotope, then remaining amount ($N$) in time $t$ is given as
$N=12.5 \%$ of $N_{0}=\dfrac{125}{1000} \times N_{0} \Rightarrow N=\dfrac{N_{0}}{8}$
We know that,
$N=N_{0}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$ where $n=\dfrac{t}{T_{1 / 2}}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{N_{0}}{8}=N_{0}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} \Rightarrow\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{3}=\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$
$\Rightarrow n=3 \Rightarrow \dfrac{t}{T_{1 / 2}}=3$
$\Rightarrow t=3 \times T_{1 / 2}=3 \times 30=90 {~h}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: An alloy is composed of two radioactive materials $A$ and $B$ having equal weight. The half life of $A$ and are  $B$  10  years and 20 years respectively. After time $t$, the alloy was found to consist of $\left(\dfrac{1}{e}\right) {kg}$ of $A$ and  $1{\text{ }}kg$  of $B$. If the atomic weight of $A$ and $B$ are same, then the value of $t$ is (Assume, $\ln 2=0.7$ )
explanation:
Given, initial amount of radioactive element $A=$ initial amount of radioactive element, $B$ i. e., ${\left( {{N_0}} \right)_A} = {\left( {{N_0}} \right)_B}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (1)$
half life of element $A, \left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_{A}=10 y r$ and half life of element $B, \left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_{B}=20 y r$
After time $t$, remaining the amount of element $A, N_{A}=\dfrac{1}{e} k g$ and remaining amount of element $B, N_{B}=1 {~kg}$
For element $A$, after time $t$, amount of element left undecayed is given as $N_{A}=\left(N_{0}\right)_{A}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{t}{\left(t_{1 / 2}\right)_{A}}}$
$\frac{1}{e} = {\left( {{N_0}} \right)_A}{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^{\frac{t}{{10}}}}{\text{ }}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (2)$
Similarly, for element $B, N_{B}=\left(N_{0}\right)_{B}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{20}}$
$\therefore \quad 1 = {\left( {{N_0}} \right)_B}{\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)^{\frac{1}{{20}}}}\quad \quad \quad \quad \quad (3)$
Dividing Eqs. (2) and (3), and using Eq. (1), we get
$\ln \dfrac{1}{e}=\dfrac{t}{20}=\ln \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$
Taking $\ln$ of both sides, we get
$\ln \dfrac{1}{e}=\dfrac{t}{20} \ln \dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{20}{\ln 2}=\dfrac{20}{0.7}=\dfrac{200}{7} y r[\because \ln 2=0.7]$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: In one average life-time of a radioactive nuclei,
explanation:
Key Idea The mean life of a radioactive element is equal to the ratio of the sum of lifetime of all the active nuclei to the total numbers of active nuclei. It can be expressed as,
Mean life, $(\tau)=\dfrac{1}{\text { decay constant }(\lambda)}$
Since, as we know that the half-life of radioactive nuclei is given as,
$T_{1 / 2}=\dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$
So, from above expression,
$T_{1 / 2}=0.693 \tau$
Hence, the correct option is (1).

options.text: more than half the active nuclei decay.
less than half the active nuclei decay

subject: Physics

question: A radioactive nucleus can decay in two different processes with half life $0.7{\text{ }}hr$  and  $0.3{\text{ }}hr.$  The effective average life of the nucleus in minutes approximately is (value of $\ln 2=0.7$)
explanation:
Key Idea If a radioactive sample has more than one radioactive material, then the effective half life.
$\dfrac{1}{T_{e f f T / 2}}=\dfrac{1}{T_{1 \dfrac{1}{2}}}+\dfrac{1}{T_{2 \dfrac{1}{2}}}+\dfrac{1}{T_{3 \dfrac{1}{3}}}+$
Where, $T_{n \dfrac{1}{2}}=$ half life of $n^{\text {th }}$ material.
For a mixture of two radioactive materials, effective half life is,
$T_{\text {eff } \dfrac{1}{2}}=\dfrac{0.7 \times 0.3}{0.7+0.3}=0.21 {hr}$
$\left(\because T_{1 \dfrac{1}{2}}=0.7 h r, T_{2 \dfrac{1}{2}}=0.3 h r\right)$
As we know, $T_{1 / 2}=0.693 \tau_{\text {mean life }}$
$ \Rightarrow \quad {\tau _{{\text{mean}}\,\,{\text{life}}}} = \frac{{{T_{{\text{eff }}\frac{1}{2}}}}}{{0.693}} = \frac{{0.21}}{{0.693}}$
$ = 0.3hr = 18\,\min .$
Hence, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Two radioactive materials $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ have decay constants $6 \lambda$ and $\lambda$, respectively. The half life of $R_{2}$ is $1.4 \times 10^{17} {~s}$. Initially they contain same number of nuclei. The time at  which the ratio of the remaining nuclei of $R_{2}$ to that of $R_{1}$ will be $e$ is (Let $\ln 2=0.7$)
explanation:
If $T_{2}$ be the half life of $R_{2}$, then
$T_{2}=\dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \Rightarrow \lambda=\dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{2}}$
Let $N_{1}$ and $N_{2}$ be the number of active nuclei of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ respectively. Also let $N_{0}$ be the initial number of active nuclei.
$\therefore N_{1}=N_{0} e^{-6 \lambda t}$ and $N_{2}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$
$\therefore \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}=\dfrac{e^{-\lambda t}}{e^{-6 \lambda t}}=e^{5 \lambda t}$
But $\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}=e$ (Given)
$\therefore 5 \lambda t=1$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{1}{5 \times \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{2}}}=\dfrac{T_{2}}{5 \ln 2}$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{1.4 \times 10^{17}}{5 \times 0.7} s=4 \times 10^{16} s$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: An active nucleus decays to $(1 / 3) r d$ in  $20{\text{ }}h.$  The fraction of original active nuclei remaining after $80 h$ is
explanation:
Decay equation, $N=N_{0} \exp (-\lambda t)$
Given, $N_{1}=N_{0} / 3, t_{1}=20 {~h}$
So, $N_{1}=N_{0} \exp \left(-\lambda t_{1}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{1}{3}=e(-20 \lambda) \Rightarrow e^{20 \lambda}=3$
Now, $N_{2}=N_{0} \exp \left(-\lambda t_{2}\right) \Rightarrow \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{0}}=e^{-80 \lambda}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{0}}=\left(e^{20 \lambda}\right)^{-4}=(3)^{-4}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{81}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: Consider a radioactive nuclide which follow decay rate given by $A(t)=A_{0} 2^{-\left(t / t_{0}\right)}$, where $A(t)$ is the decay rate after time $t$ and the initial decay rate $A_{0}$ at zero time. Let $A_{1}$ be the  fraction of original activity which remains after 120 hours. Likewise $A_{2}$ is the  fraction of original activity remaining after 200 hours. If $A_{1} / A_{2}=16$, then the half-life $\left(t_{0}\right)$ will be
explanation:
Given, decay rate, $A=A_{0} 2^{-\left(\dfrac{t}{t_{0}}\right)}$
Let $\dfrac{t}{t_{0}}=n$ where, $t_{0}=$ half life.
As, $\dfrac{A_{1}}{A_{2}}=16 \Rightarrow \dfrac{A_{0} \cdot 2^{-n_{1}}}{A_{0} \cdot 2^{-n_{2}}}=16$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2^{n_{2}-n_{1}}=16=2^{4}$
$\Rightarrow n_{2}-n_{1}=4 \Rightarrow \dfrac{t_{2}}{t_{0}}-\dfrac{t_{1}}{t_{0}}=4$
As, $t_{1}=120 h$ and $t_{2}=200 h$,
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{200}{t_{0}}-\dfrac{120}{t_{0}}=4 \Rightarrow \dfrac{80}{t_{0}}=4$
$\Rightarrow \quad t_{0}=\dfrac{80}{4}=20 {~h}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: In uranium radioactive series, initial nucleus ${ }^{238} U_{92}$ decays to final nucleus ${ }^{206} {~Pb}_{82}$ In this process, the number of $\alpha$ - particles and $\beta$ particles emitted are
explanation:
In decay, ${ }_{92}^{238} {U} \rightarrow{ }_{82}^{206} {~Pb}$
Mass number reduces by $\Delta A=32=4 \times 8$ and proton number is reduced by
$\Delta Z=10=2 \times 8-6$
So, $8 \alpha$-particles and $6 \beta$-particles must be emitted.
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: The half-life of ${ }_{84}^{209} {Po}$ is 103 years. The time it takes for  $100{\text{ }}g$  sample of    ${ }_{84}^{209} {Po}$    to decay to    $3.125{\text{ }}g$   is
explanation:
In one half life, half of amount is decayed. So, decay occurs as, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958693/preptracker/questions/q_111982_explanation_1764958692778.png So, time taken $=103 \times 5=515 y r$.
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question:
In a radioactive decay, decay constant of element 2 is three times that of element 1. Find the ratio of numbers of nuclei of element 1 to element 2 after one half-life of 2.
(Assume initial numbers of nuclei are the same for both elements)
explanation:
For element 1: $N_{1}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda_{1} t}$
For element 2: $N_{2}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda_{2} t}$
Let ${\lambda _1} = \lambda $ & ${\lambda _2} = 3\lambda $
Therefore, $\dfrac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}=e^{-\left(\lambda_{1}-\lambda_{2}\right) t}$
Given that the time $t=\dfrac{\ln 2}{\lambda_{2}}=\dfrac{\ln 2}{3 \lambda}$
$\frac{{{N_1}}}{{{N_2}}} = {e^{ - (\lambda - 3\lambda )}}\frac{{\ln 2}}{{3\lambda }} = {e^{\frac{2}{3}\ln 2}} = {2^{\frac{2}{3}}}$
subject: Physics

question:
In a hypothetical fission reaction,
${ }_{92} X^{236} \rightarrow_{56} Y^{141}+{ }_{36} Z^{92}+3 R$
The identity of emitted particles $(R)$ is
explanation:
$92=56+36+Z_{R} \Rightarrow Z_{R}=0$
$\therefore \quad R$ is a neutral particle.
$A_{R}=\dfrac{236-(141+92)}{3}=1$
So, the emitted particle $(R)$ is neutron.
subject: Physics

question: The activity of a radioactive element decreases to one third of the original activity ${A_{0}}$ in a period of nine years. After a further lapse of 9 years, if activity is found to be ${\dfrac{A_{0}}{N}}$. Find the value of ${N}$.
explanation:
$\dfrac{A_{0}}{3}=A_{0}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{9}{T_{1 / 2}}}$
$\dfrac{A_{0}}{3}=A_{0}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{9}{T_{1 / 2}}}$
$A^{\prime}=\dfrac{A_{0}}{3}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{9}{T_{1 / 2}}}$
Dividing $\dfrac{A^{\prime} \times 3}{A_{0}}=\dfrac{1}{3}$ or $A^{\prime}=\dfrac{A_{0}}{9}$
Hence $N=9$.
subject: Physics

question: A human body excretes (removes by waste discharge, sweating etc.) certain materials by a law similar to radioactivity. If technetium is injected in some form in a human body, the body excretes half the amount in 24 hours. A patient is given an injection containing ${{ }^{99} {Tc}}$. This isotope is radioactive with a half-life of 6 hours. The activity from the body just after the injection is $6\,\mu Ci.$ How much time (in hours) will elapse before the activity falls to $3\,\mu Ci$ ?
explanation:
If in time $t$ total atoms inside the body, $N_{\text {body }}=N_{0}(2)^{-t / 2 t}$
If after time $t, N_{A}=\dfrac{N_{0}}{2}$
Total active atoms inside body at time $t,$
${N_A} = {N_{{\text{body }}}}{(2)^{ - t/6}}$
If after time $t$,$N_{A}=\dfrac{N_{0}}{2}$
$\frac{{{N_0}}}{2} = {N_0}{(2)^{ - t/24}} \cdot {(2)^{t/6}}$
$ \Rightarrow t\left( {\frac{1}{{24}} + \frac{1}{6}} \right) = 1$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{24}{5}=4.8 {hrs}$
subject: Physics

question: The mean lives of a radioactive substance are 1620 years and 405 years for ${\alpha}$-emission and ${\beta}$-emission respectively. Find out the time (in years) during which three fourth of a sample will decay if it is decaying both by ${\alpha}$-emission and ${\beta}$-emission simultaneously. (Given: ${\ln 2=0.693}$ )
explanation:
The decay constants for $\alpha$ and $\beta$ emissions are $1 / 1620$ and $1 / 405$ per year respectively.
In this case effective decay constant for both decays simultaneously is
$\lambda=\lambda_{\alpha}+\lambda_{\beta}$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=\dfrac{1}{1620}+\dfrac{1}{405}=\dfrac{1}{324}$ year $^{-1}$
Let $t$ be time in which the given sample decays three fourth.
Therefore, the fraction of sample undecayed in time $t$ is $1 / 4$. Hence
$N=i_{v_{0}} / 4$
Now from decay equation
$N=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t} \Rightarrow \dfrac{N_{0}}{4}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{\ln (4)}{\lambda}=1.386 \times 324=449\,\, {yr}$
subject: Physics

question: A nucleus of ${U_{x_{1}}}$ has a half-life of 24.1 days. How long   a sample of ${U_{x_{1}}}$ take to change to ${90 \%}$ of it to ${U_{x_{2}}}$ ?
